source: On The Tape / RiskReversal — Fear is the market killer
summary: Many tech companies that announced 2022 'investment years' will walk back those plans in Q1 earnings due to stock price pressure and talent retention concerns
summary_extended: Baker argues that many tech companies which announced aggressive 2022 'investment years' during the Oct-Dec 2021 period will be forced to reverse those plans when reporting Q1 2022 earnings. His reasoning is multi-layered: stocks in the sector are already down 50-60% from when those plans were announced, the market has demonstrated zero tolerance for investment-year narratives, employee morale is suffering as stock-based compensation has lost value and options are deeply underwater, creating retention risk that undermines the very talent-driven rationale for increased spending. The investment implication is that investors should expect margin expansion and capex/opex rollbacks across the broader software and SaaS universe as management teams capitulate to market pressure, making these companies more attractive on a near-term profitability basis. Baker carves out one exception: large banks increasing tech spending out of fear of fintech disruption, whose plans he believes will persist regardless of market conditions. No specific company names were cited; the thesis is a broad sector-level structural call on software and SaaS operating behavior.
detail: Baker tweeted and elaborates that tech companies that set aggressive 2022 investment/capex plans in Oct-Dec 2021 will be compelled to reverse them. Reasons: stocks already down 50-60% since plans were set, market has zero tolerance for investment years, employee morale and retention suffer when stock is down, options are underwater. He expects option repricing and plan rollbacks. Exception: large banks spending on tech out of fear of fintech — those plans likely persist.
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly tweeted this view and elaborates with specific mechanisms. He says 'I do think a lot of tech companies...will talk those plans back in Q1.'
quote_fragment: a majority of companies that announce plans for 2022 to be an investment year...will talk those plans back
